sound_type: the quack -- the female's loud descrescendo series 'QUACK-quack-quack-quack' and the male's softer nasal 'raeb'
ecosystem: lowland pond, urban park, marsh, lake edge, estuary, river -- nearly every freshwater habitat

csv_substitution.reason: SPS Part B engine is birds-and-mammals only. Mallard replaces Bullfrog, preserving the original's lowland pond habitat and E=5 energy level. The Bullfrog is an invasive species in the Pacific Northwest with a deep resonant 'jug-o-rum' call that dominates the pond soundscape (E=5, Ranidae). The Mallard dominates the same pond soundscape with its quack (E=5, Anatidae). Both are ubiquitous lowland pond species and the loudest voice in their respective taxonomic groups. The Bullfrog is invasive; the Mallard is native but so common it becomes almost invisible. Fifteenth consecutive CSV substitution replacing an amphibian-class species (Pacific Chorus Frog at 45, Columbia Spotted Frog at 46, Red-legged Frog at 47, Tailed Frog at 48, Western Toad at 49, Rough-skinned Newt at 50, Northwestern Salamander at 51, Long-toed Salamander at 52, Olympic Torrent Salamander at 53, Clouded Salamander at 54, Oregon Slender Salamander at 55, Ensatina at 56, Pacific Giant Salamander at 57, Red-legged Frog at 58, Bullfrog at 59).

coordinates.description: Seattle reference; Mallards are ubiquitous throughout western Washington at every freshwater habitat from sea level to approximately 1,500 meters. Present in every county, breeding in every city with standing water. Found at Green Lake, Union Bay Natural Area, Seward Park, Washington Park Arboretum, and every Seattle-area waterway, pond, lake, marsh, estuary, and river. The widest habitat distribution of any species in the engine.

taxonomy_note.description: Mallard is the 2nd ANATIDAE in the engine -- back-to-back with Wood Duck at degree 58. UNPRECEDENTED consecutive same-family entries with maximally divergent ecological strategies. Genus Anas is NEW to the engine at degree 59 -- the core genus of dabbling ducks (~30 species worldwide). Anas from Latin anas (duck). platyrhynchos from Greek platys (broad, flat) + rhynchos (bill). Linnaeus 1758. Passeriformes percentage drops to 81.7% (49 of 60 entries). Fourth consecutive non-passerine in five degrees (55 Passeriformes, 56 Columbiformes, 57 Coraciiformes, 58 Anseriformes, 59 Anseriformes).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Anas = Latin anas, meaning simply 'duck.' platyrhynchos = Ancient Greek platys (broad, flat) + rhynchos (bill, beak). Full translation: 'flat-billed duck.' Named by Linnaeus in 1758. The most direct, unadorned taxonomic name possible -- where the Wood Duck's Aix sponsa ('Aristotle's waterfowl in bridal dress') reaches into mythology and aesthetics, the Mallard's name reaches only to the obvious: a duck with a flat bill.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Anas is new to the engine at degree 59. Contains approximately 30 species worldwide -- the core radiation of dabbling ducks. Where Aix (degree 58) is an exclusive genus of two spectacularly plumaged species, Anas is the genus of generalists, the workhorses of Anatidae. The type species of the genus is the Mallard itself. Anas is the largest duck genus to enter the engine.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes appears for the 2nd time at degree 59. Established at degree 58 (Wood Duck). Back-to-back Anseriformes entries. After degree 59: 49 of 60 entries (81.7%) are Passeriformes -- dropping below 82% for the first time. The non-passerine edge continues to grow: four non-passerine orders in five degrees (55-59).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae reaches 2 members at degree 59 (Wood Duck 58, Mallard 59). Back-to-back entries from the same family -- unprecedented with such divergent ecological strategies. The family now demonstrates adaptive radiation: two species in the same family occupying opposite ends of the specialization-generalism spectrum. 25 families across 60 entries.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Female descrescendo quack: 800-2,500 Hz, falling in volume and pitch over 5-10 notes. Male raeb: 1,000-3,000 Hz, soft nasal grunt. Male grunt-whistle: 1,500-4,000 Hz during courtship. Both sexes' vocalizations are innate.
song_metrics.note_structure: DESCRESCENDO SERIES: The female's primary call is a series of 5-10 harsh quack notes that decrease in volume and pitch -- the first note is loudest and highest, each subsequent note softer and lower. The descrescendo pattern is diagnostic for Mallard. The male's raeb is a single short nasal note. Neither sex produces the thin whistle of the Wood Duck.
song_metrics.note_types: LIMITED vocalization types but high acoustic impact. Female descrescendo quack (contact/alarm/assembly), female inciting call (pair formation), male raeb (contact/courtship), male grunt-whistle (courtship display), male head-up-tail-up whistle (courtship display), alarm calls. Vocalizations are innate -- no cultural learning, no geographic dialects.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: The descrescendo quack is the phrase: a series of notes that begins loud and fades. The series is the structure -- no variation within a series, no melodic development, no tonal complexity. The phrase says everything in its first note and then retreats. Structurally the inverse of the Wood Duck's ascending whistle, which rises toward its climax.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh. Nasal. Resonant. Penetrating. Broad-spectrum with rich harmonics. The opposite of the Wood Duck's thin, breathy whistle. The quack covers a wide frequency band -- it is not a pure tone but a complex waveform with significant harmonic content. The timbre carries well across open water and through urban noise.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- loud, persistent, projecting. Audible at 500+ meters across open water. The loudest Anatidae vocalization in PNW freshwater environments. The first E=5 since American Goldfinch at degree 43 -- a 16-degree gap. The +2 energy jump from Wood Duck (E=3) is the largest since degree 42 to 43.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate tempo within the descrescendo series -- approximately 2-4 quacks per second. Series repeated at irregular intervals. The calling pattern is event-driven (alarm, contact, assembly) rather than rhythmic or sustained. At dawn and dusk, multiple females quacking simultaneously create a dense acoustic texture across the pond surface.
song_metrics.context: Female descrescendo quack given for contact, alarm, assembly of ducklings, and general communication. Female inciting call given during pair formation. Male raeb given for contact and during courtship. Male grunt-whistle and head-up-tail-up given during ritualized courtship displays. Vocalizations occur year-round, most intense during fall-winter pair formation and spring breeding.
song_metrics.regional_variation: MINIMAL geographic variation. Vocalizations are innate. No dialects, no cultural learning. Like the Wood Duck's whistle (degree 58), the Belted Kingfisher's rattle (degree 57), and the Band-tailed Pigeon's coo (degree 56), the Mallard's quack is genetically determined. Four consecutive non-passerine species (56-59), all with innate vocalizations.
song_metrics.key_property: THE QUACK -- the 60th acoustic element (species side). The world's most recognized duck call -- the female Mallard's descrescendo series that IS the sound of 'duck' in human culture. Named simply 'the quack' because it needs no further qualification. Three resonances: (1) ubiquity -- heard in every freshwater habitat in the PNW; (2) descrescendo -- the fourth directional character after descending coo (56), flat rattle (57), ascending whistle (58); (3) recognition -- the only acoustic element the audience already owns. The engine's 60th element is not a discovery but a rediscovery.

plumage.overall: Medium-to-large dabbling duck, larger than Wood Duck. Male: glossy dark green head, white neck collar, chestnut breast, pale gray body, black rump, curling drake tail feathers -- the most recognizable duck plumage in the world. Female: mottled brown throughout -- camouflage for ground nesting.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: MALE: Glossy dark green head (iridescent) with no crest, white neck collar, yellow bill with black nail. FEMALE: Mottled brown with orange bill showing dark saddle markings. Both sexes: iridescent blue-purple speculum bordered by white on secondary feathers.
plumage.upperparts: Male: pale gray with fine vermiculated patterning. Female: mottled dark brown with lighter feather edges.
plumage.underparts: Male: pale gray belly, chestnut-brown breast sharply demarcated. Female: warm brown, mottled throughout.
plumage.head: Male: glossy dark green, appearing black in shade. Iridescent in sunlight. No crest. White neck ring separating green head from chestnut breast. Female: warm brown with darker crown and eyeline, pale supercilium. No eye ring (contrast with female Wood Duck's white teardrop).
plumage.bill: Male: bright yellow to olive-yellow with black nail at tip. Female: orange with dark saddle markings. Both sexes show the broad, flat, spatulate bill typical of dabbling ducks -- the 'platyrhynchos' of the scientific name.
plumage.tail: Male: dark with distinctive upward-curling central tail feathers ('drake curl') -- a small, jaunty diagnostic feature. Female: dark brown, typical duck shape.
plumage.feet: Orange. Webbed. Standard Anatidae feet without the sharp claws of the Wood Duck's tree-climbing adaptations.
plumage.field_id: Medium-to-large dabbling duck (50-65 cm, 750-1,580 g). Male unmistakable: glossy green head, white neck collar, chestnut breast, gray body, yellow bill, drake curl. Female: mottled brown, orange bill with dark saddle, blue-purple speculum. Found on every type of freshwater habitat. In flight: broad wings, blue-purple speculum with white borders, rapid wingbeat. The default duck -- the one everyone recognizes.

nodes.title: The Quack -- 60th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: The world's most recognized duck call -- the female Mallard's descrescendo series 'QUACK-quack-quack-quack' falling in volume and pitch over 2-5 seconds. E=5. When people say 'ducks go quack,' they mean the female Mallard. Named simply 'the quack' because it needs no further qualification. The only acoustic element the audience already knows. Fourth directional character (descending coo, flat rattle, ascending whistle, descrescendo series). Music-theory parallel: the fortissimo entrance of a theme everyone already hums -- the sound that arrives carrying cultural weight before a single note is explained.

nodes.title: Back-to-Back Anatidae -- Unprecedented
nodes.description: Wood Duck (58) then Mallard (59) -- two consecutive species from the same family with maximally divergent ecological strategies. Hidden aristocrat (E=3, cavity nester, wooded ponds, rising whistle) versus ubiquitous generalist (E=5, ground nester, all freshwater, descrescendo quack). Baroque versus modernist. The first consecutive same-family pair in the engine's history that illuminates adaptive radiation through contrast. Music-theory parallel: theme and variation -- the same harmonic framework (Anatidae, Anseriformes, freshwater, dabbling duck) with completely different orchestration.

nodes.title: Back-to-Back Dimorphism -- Two Aesthetic Strategies
nodes.description: Two consecutive Anatidae, both with extreme sexual dimorphism, but fundamentally different aesthetics. Wood Duck = baroque ornamentation (iridescent multi-color, patterned, edged, spotted -- a jeweler's creation). Mallard = graphic boldness (green/chestnut/gray in clean blocks -- a graphic designer's creation). Both demonstrate that sexual selection drives male ornamentation, but through different evolutionary solutions. The engine documents two answers to the same question in two consecutive degrees.

nodes.title: The Green Head -- Icon
nodes.description: The male Mallard's glossy dark green head is the single most culturally reproduced plumage pattern of any wild bird on Earth. Appearing on logos, signage, hunting gear, children's illustrations, and the universal concept of 'duck.' The drake curl -- two central tail feathers curling upward -- is diagnostic at any distance. The green head has become shorthand for 'duck' worldwide. Where the Wood Duck's beauty is hidden and discovered, the Mallard's beauty is displayed and overlooked.

nodes.title: Ancestor of Domestic Ducks
nodes.description: The Mallard is the wild ancestor of nearly all domestic duck breeds -- Pekin, Rouen, Khaki Campbell, Indian Runner, and dozens of others. Domesticated approximately 4,000-2,000 years ago in East Asia and independently in Europe. Billions of domestic ducks worldwide carry Mallard genetics. The species' genetic legacy is embedded in global agriculture. No other species in the engine has generated an entire branch of human agriculture from its wild genome. Music-theory parallel: the folk melody that became a national anthem -- a wild theme domesticated, elaborated, and deployed at industrial scale.

nodes.title: The Hybridization Threat -- Genetic Absorption
nodes.description: The Mallard hybridizes with at least 40 other duck species. This genetic promiscuity threatens American Black Duck, Hawaiian Duck (fewer than 2,500 pure individuals), Mottled Duck, and Mexican Duck through progressive genetic absorption. The most successful duck in the Northern Hemisphere threatens its closest relatives through its very success. Abundance as a weapon -- the generalist assimilates the specialist. A conservation dynamic the engine has not encountered before: a species whose threat to biodiversity comes not from predation or habitat competition but from genetic swamping. Music-theory parallel: the dominant modulation -- a key so powerful it absorbs adjacent tonalities into itself.

nodes.title: E=5 Returns -- 16-Degree Gap Closes
nodes.description: First E=5 species since American Goldfinch at degree 43. Sixteen degrees at E=1 to E=4. The energy jump from Wood Duck (E=3) to Mallard (E=5) is +2 -- the largest single-degree jump since degrees 42 to 43. The engine's two E=5 species: Goldfinch at 12 grams and Mallard at 1,100 grams. Maximum acoustic energy spans the full body-mass range, from the lightest songbird to a heavy waterfowl.

nodes.title: The Familiarity Challenge -- Making the Known Visible
nodes.description: The first species the entire audience already knows. Every prior degree (0-58) required some introduction. Degree 59 requires reintroduction -- making the familiar visible again. The engine's work reverses: not 'here is something you have never seen' but 'look again at what you have always seen.' The Mallard's ubiquity has made it invisible. A Mallard on a city park pond is background. The challenge is to transform background into foreground.

nodes.title: The Widest Habitat Range in the Engine
nodes.description: The Mallard occupies every freshwater habitat from urban drainage ditches to mountain lakes at 1,500 meters. Urban park ponds, lowland marshes, lakes, reservoirs, rivers, estuaries, agricultural ponds, suburban retention basins, golf course water features. No other species in the engine approaches this habitat breadth. Where every prior species maps a specific habitat type, the Mallard maps all of them. The acoustic generalist that fills every available freshwater niche.

nodes.title: Lorenz and Ethology -- The Model Organism
nodes.description: Konrad Lorenz used Mallards for groundbreaking research on imprinting and fixed action patterns, contributing to his 1973 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Mallard's courtship displays (grunt-whistle, head-up-tail-up, nod-swimming, bridling) were among the first complex animal behaviors described with experimental scientific rigor. More scientific papers have been published on Mallard behavior, ecology, and genetics than on any other duck species. The Mallard is to waterfowl biology what the fruit fly is to genetics -- the model organism that built the field.

nodes.title: Four Freshwater Species in Eight Degrees
nodes.description: Degrees 52, 57, 58, and 59 give the engine four freshwater specialists spanning the complete hydrological gradient. American Dipper (degree 52): fast mountain torrents, invertebrate monitor. Belted Kingfisher (degree 57): cold streams, fish monitor. Wood Duck (degree 58): still wooded ponds, forest-water bridge. Mallard (degree 59): all freshwater habitats, generalist. Mountain torrent to cold stream to wooded pond to every pond. The engine maps the complete freshwater landscape through four species.

nodes.title: The Long Winters Parallel -- Familiarity and Depth
nodes.description: Degree 59 pairs the Mallard with The Long Winters. Both are the most visible, most accessible representative of their category. John Roderick is the most publicly visible Barsuk artist -- known for talking about music as much as making it. The Mallard is the most visible Anatidae -- known for being 'the duck in the park' as much as for its ecological role. Closes the Barsuk Records trio (57-58-59) simultaneously with the freshwater trio (57-58-59). Both entities use accessibility as strategy: Long Winters' hooks pull you in; Mallard's visibility keeps it in human consciousness. Back-to-back shifts from intimacy (58) to ubiquity (59) in both Part A and Part B.

nodes.title: The Anchor Species -- Ecological Foundation
nodes.description: As the most abundant dabbling duck in the Northern Hemisphere (~19 million in North America), the Mallard functions as an anchor species in wetland ecology. Seed dispersal connecting isolated wetlands. Nutrient cycling between aquatic and terrestrial systems. Prey base for raccoons, foxes, coyotes, mink, eagles, hawks, and owls. Population indicator for wetland health. Disease vector for avian influenza with continental epidemiological significance. The Mallard is simultaneously conservation success (abundant, resilient, sustainably harvested) and conservation problem (hybridization, disease, competitive dominance).

nodes.title: Fifteenth Consecutive CSV Substitution
nodes.description: Bullfrog (Lithobates catesbeianus, invasive) replaced by Mallard. The fifteenth consecutive amphibian-class substitution (degrees 45-59). The longest unbroken substitution streak in the engine. The original CSV remains heavily amphibian in the lowland to mid-elevation zone. Note: unlike previous substitutions which replaced native amphibians, degree 59 replaces an invasive species -- the first invasive species in the original CSV to be substituted.

cross_references.relationship: BACK-TO-BACK ANATIDAE -- UNPRECEDENTED. The pond whistle (degree 58, Aix sponsa, E=3, hidden pond specialist, tree-cavity nester, rising whistle) yields to the quack (degree 59, Anas platyrhynchos, E=5, ubiquitous generalist, ground nester, descrescendo quack). Same family, same order, maximally divergent ecological strategy. Hidden beauty versus universal visibility. Baroque ornamentation versus graphic boldness. The first consecutive same-family pair in the engine's history with such complete ecological divergence.

THREE CONSECUTIVE AQUATIC-ASSOCIATED SPECIES (57-58-59). Stream diver (degree 57, Coraciiformes, E=3, cold streams, harsh rattle) to pond swimmer (degree 58, Anseriformes, E=3, wooded ponds, rising whistle) to pond generalist (degree 59, Anseriformes, E=5, all freshwater, descrescendo quack). The freshwater trio closes at degree 59. Three degrees, two orders, three fundamentally different acoustic characters, spanning the freshwater gradient from cold stream to every pond.

E=5 RETURNS AFTER 16-DEGREE GAP. Last E=5 was Goldfinch at degree 43. The engine's two E=5 species: Goldfinch at 12 grams and Mallard at 1,100 grams. Maximum acoustic energy at opposite ends of the body-mass spectrum, demonstrating that E=5 is independent of body size -- a 12g passerine and a 1,100g waterfowl both achieve maximum energy through different vocal mechanisms.

TWO UBIQUITOUS SPECIES -- both the 'default' of their taxonomic group. Robin = default passerine, the bird non-birders can name. Mallard = default duck, the duck non-birders can name. Both are year-round residents, habitat generalists, and deeply embedded in human culture. Together they bracket avian familiarity from two different orders: Passeriformes and Anseriformes.

WETLAND GUILD EXPANSION. The heron wades (degree 0/14, patience as strategy), the Wood Duck swims hidden (degree 58, concealment as strategy), the Mallard swims everywhere (degree 59, ubiquity as strategy). Three wetland strategies from three orders. The engine's wetland coverage deepens: wading predator, specialist swimmer, generalist swimmer.

FOUR FRESHWATER SPECIES in eight degrees (52, 57, 58, 59). American Dipper (fast mountain torrents, invertebrate monitor), Belted Kingfisher (cold streams, fish monitor), Wood Duck (still wooded ponds, forest-water bridge), Mallard (all freshwater habitats, generalist). The engine maps the complete freshwater gradient from mountain torrent to urban park pond through four species spanning the entire hydrological spectrum.

Mallard breeds and winters throughout western Washington at every freshwater habitat. Year-round resident. The most common and most visible duck in the PNW. Ground nester in dense vegetation. Second Anatidae in the engine (after Wood Duck). Most commonly hunted and most commonly banded duck in Washington State.

Habitat generalist spanning urban to wild freshwater systems. Seed dispersal agent connecting isolated wetlands. Nutrient cycling between aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Wild ancestor of domestic ducks -- genetic legacy embedded in global agriculture. Hybridization threat to closely related species through genetic absorption. Prey base for multiple predator guilds. Avian influenza carrier with continental epidemiological significance.
